sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
The patient reports fatigue and abdominal pain.
History of Present Illness
The patient reports fatigue for the past two months, accompanied by abdominal pain in the mid-abdomen, just below the belly button. He describes a nagging feeling in his stomach that varies, and reports constipation and explosive diarrhea. The patient also notes a sensitive stomach, particularly to pasta with creamy sauces, with a bowel movement frequency of approximately three to four times per week when constipated. The pain improves when he massages his abdomen. He denies nausea, vomiting, heartburn, or indigestion. He reports stable weight, with a desire to lose approximately 25 pounds. He denies any family history of gastrointestinal issues, but reports his sister has irritable bowel syndrome. He denies any family history of gastrointestinal cancer or liver disease. He reports no previous surgeries on his abdomen and states his gallbladder and appendix are intact. He reports having had a colonoscopy when he turns 50 years old.
Findings

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
The clinician hears bowel sounds in all four quadrants of the patient's abdomen. The clinician notes stool in the patient's lower colon and a slight enlargement of the liver. The patient's primary care physician reported slightly elevated liver enzymes (ALT, AST, ALP) four months prior. The clinician notes no jaundice in the patient's eyes and skin. The clinician notes normal lung sounds and heart sounds with no murmurs.
Assessment
The patient presents with symptoms suggestive of a gastrointestinal disorder, potentially including irritable bowel syndrome or other functional bowel issues.
Plan
Order a cervical MRI to evaluate for cervical radiculopathy. Counsel the patient on dietary modifications to manage his symptoms, including avoiding trigger foods and increasing fiber intake. Continue the patient on Crestor and olmesartan, and advise him to continue Tylenol as needed for pain. Schedule a follow-up appointment in two weeks to assess response to treatment and further investigate findings.
